title: The Deal Takes Shape: Trump Claims Final-Stage Negotiations with Iran as Hormuz Control Solidifies
subhead: President Trump announced that US-Iran negotiations have entered the 'final stage' and a deal could be reached within days. But on the ground, Iran has consolidated de facto control of the Strait of Hormuz — imposing checkpoints, diplomatic clearances, and in some cases fees on vessels transiting the world's most critical oil chokepoint.

sections.heading: Trump's Deal Timeline
sections.body:
President Donald Trump stated publicly on Wednesday that negotiations with Iran are in the 'final stage' and could conclude within days. He simultaneously warned that the US is 'in no hurry' and threatened that if talks fail, the US would 'do something nasty' — a continuation of his dual-track approach combining diplomatic engagement with military threats.

The rhetoric has moved markets: oil prices fell 4% on expectations that a deal may be imminent. But the signals remain mixed. Trump also claimed that Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu will 'do whatever I want' regarding Iran — an assertion that raises questions about how much flexibility Netanyahu actually has, and whether Israeli policy will align with whatever emerges from US-Iran negotiations.
sections.whyItMatters: The gap between 'final stage' rhetoric and on-the-ground reality remains significant. Iran's Revolutionary Guard has threatened to 'extend the war beyond the region' if attacks resume. A deal may be taking shape, but the leverage equation is fluid, and hardliners in both Tehran and Washington could still disrupt progress.

sections.heading: Iran's Hormuz Checkpoint System
sections.body:
A Reuters investigation reveals that Iran has implemented a multi-tiered clearance system for vessels passing through the Strait of Hormuz. The system involves government-to-government arrangements, intense vetting by Iranian authorities, and in some cases fees for passage. Iranian officials claim they coordinated 26 ships through the strait in the past 24 hours alone.

The US has warned nations against complying with Iran's controls, but some shippers and governments are taking the risk anyway — desperate to move energy supplies as global inventories draw down. The implications are clear: Tehran is not just blocking Hormuz, it is building a bureaucratic architecture for controlling it.
sections.whyItMatters: This is a structural change. Even if a ceasefire holds and formal hostilities end, Iran has demonstrated that it can impose a checkpoint regime on global shipping. The legal and security implications will outlast any single negotiation.

sections.heading: Senate Rebukes Trump on War Powers
sections.body:
The US Senate advanced a measure to constrain Trump's ability to wage war against Iran without congressional approval — a rare rebuke from a Republican-controlled chamber. The vote came just one day after Republican Senator Bill Cassidy of Louisiana survived a primary challenge, only to immediately buck his party's leader on this issue.

The move reflects growing war fatigue among the American public and within Congress itself. Support for military action against Iran has always been contingent on the operation being limited and successful. As the conflict has stretched on without clear resolution, congressional patience has frayed.
sections.whyItMatters: Trump's freedom to escalate militarily is narrowing even as he publicly threatens further strikes. The Senate vote signals that continued war without a negotiated outcome risks losing Republican support — a constraint that may be pushing Trump toward deal-making.

sections.heading: Putin and Xi: Strategic Ties on Display
sections.body:
Vladimir Putin met Xi Jinping in Beijing on Wednesday, just days after Donald Trump's own high-profile visit to the Chinese capital. The optics were carefully choreographed: Xi welcomed Putin as an 'old friend' and the two leaders emphasized their 'unyielding' strategic partnership. Energy trade featured prominently, with Russia seeking to expand oil and gas exports to China as Persian Gulf disruptions create market opportunities.

The timing is deliberate. Putin's visit follows Trump's Beijing summit by less than a week, projecting an image of China as the indispensable power engaging both Washington and Moscow. Xi also called for an 'immediate end' to the West Asia conflict — a signal of Beijing's interest in stability along its energy supply routes.
sections.whyItMatters: China is positioning itself as the pivot point in great power competition — engaging Trump on trade and Putin on energy security simultaneously. Xi's ability to maintain working relationships with both leaders gives Beijing leverage that neither Washington nor Moscow can easily counter.

sections.heading: Ukraine: Zelenskyy Warns of Northern Offensive
sections.body:
Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy stated that intelligence indicates Russia is planning a new offensive in northern Ukraine, potentially targeting the Chernihiv-Kyiv direction. The warning comes amid continued fighting in eastern Ukraine and follows a Russian drone attack on Kyiv that killed nine people.

The timing is notable: Putin is scheduled to meet Trump in Alaska on Friday for talks on ending the war. A new offensive in the north could be leverage-building, or it could reflect that Putin faces pressure from hardliners to show strength even as he seeks a diplomatic off-ramp.
sections.whyItMatters: The Ukraine war does not pause while Putin negotiates. If anything, military pressure on the ground creates bargaining chips for diplomatic talks — but also risks escalation that could derail negotiations entirely.

sections.heading: Europe Moves on Critical Minerals
sections.body:
The European Union has shortlisted tungsten, rare earths, and gallium for its first joint stockpile of critical minerals — a direct response to dependency on Chinese supplies. The move comes as global supply chain vulnerabilities have been exposed by the Iran crisis and broader geopolitical tensions.

The initiative is modest in scale but symbolic in intent: Europe is trying to reduce the leverage that concentrated mineral supply chains give to China. The EU joins the US and other powers in attempting to build resilience into strategic commodity supplies.
sections.whyItMatters: The critical minerals race is accelerating. Every major power now recognizes that access to rare earths, tungsten, and advanced materials is a national security priority. The Iran conflict has added urgency to supply chain diversification efforts that were already underway.

bottomLine: A US-Iran deal appears closer today, but the terms remain unclear and Iran has already built a checkpoint architecture over Hormuz that may outlast any ceasefire. Trump says negotiations are in their 'final stage,' the Senate has voted to constrain his war powers, and Putin met Xi in Beijing to showcase a partnership that is increasingly shaped by Chinese leverage. Meanwhile, Zelenskyy warns of a new Russian offensive in northern Ukraine ahead of Friday's Trump-Putin summit, and Europe moves to build its first critical minerals stockpile. The day's developments point toward diplomatic movement on multiple fronts — but movement is not resolution, and the structures of leverage continue to shift.
